aliases: Malay Chicken Stew with Kurma Spices

english: Malay Chicken Stew with Kurma Spices

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Ayam Masak Kurma; 'chicken stew' (FOODON_03315671) is the closest defensible match for this slow-simmered spiced chicken dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Ayam Masak Kurma. 'Chicken meat, cooked' (A0FJP) is used as the closest defensible anchor; the dish includes coconut milk, spices, and vegetables.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this traditional Malay dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Ayam Masak Kurma is approximately 250 g, comprising a portion of chicken pieces with gravy and vegetables, as typically served at a Malaysian or Singaporean meal.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Chicken curry with coconut milk (adapted for Ayam Masak Kurma)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Ayam Masak Kurma is not listed as a discrete entry in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database. Values are adapted from the HPB entry for Malay-style chicken curry with coconut milk, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for similar coconut-milk chicken stews. The input record's values (energy 155 kcal, protein 9.8 g, fat 10.5 g, carbohydrate 5.2 g per 100 g) are consistent with this source range and are retained. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 5.5 g per 100 g based on the coconut milk content typical of this dish, consistent with HPB and MyFCD reference ranges.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Dishes and Curries — analogous estimate based on chicken korma / mild coconut-milk chicken curry
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ayam Masak Kurma exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with mild coconut-milk chicken curry / chicken korma entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The high protein (9.8 g) and fat (10.5 g) content from chicken and coconut milk significantly attenuate postprandial glucose response from the modest carbohydrate load (5.2 g per 100 g, primarily from potatoes and vegetables). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 13.0) / 100 = 5.9 (serving carbohydrate: 5.2 g/100 g × 250 g = 13.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (280 mg per 100 g; ~700 mg per 250 g serving) and saturated fat from coconut milk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to control sodium intake (~700 mg per serving), request reduced-salt preparation, and be mindful of the saturated fat from coconut milk, which may contribute to arterial stiffness; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables can partially offset sodium effects.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) with high protein and fat attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ayam Masak Kurma has a low estimated GI (~45) and a low glycemic load per serving (~5.9), making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served with a low-GI carbohydrate such as basmati rice; portion control of potato content within the dish is advised to further moderate carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~5.5 g per 100 g) contributing to LDL cholesterol elevation risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content from coconut milk (~13.75 g per 250 g serving) is a concern for cardiovascular disease management; individuals should limit frequency of consumption, choose smaller portions, and consider requesting reduced coconut milk or substituting with low-fat yogurt to lower saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, cinnamon, coriander) balanced against saturated fat from coconut milk
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The kurma spice blend including ginger and cinnamon provides anti-inflammatory phytochemicals that may benefit rheumatoid arthritis; however, the saturated fat from coconut milk should be moderated as excess saturated fat may promote systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Masak Kurma is a mild, aromatic, and creamy Malaysian and Singaporean chicken stew deeply influenced by Indian korma. The dish is distinguished from other Malay curries by its non-spicy, pale yellowish-white appearance and gentle, fragrant flavour profile. Chicken pieces are slowly simmered in a rich gravy built from a blended spice paste of shallots, garlic, and ginger, enriched with the classic 'kurma' spice blend comprising coriander, cumin, fennel, cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom. The sauce is thickened and enriched with coconut milk or yogurt, and sometimes ground cashews for additional body and richness. Potatoes, carrots, and large pieces of onion are common additions. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 155 kcal, 9.8 g protein, 10.5 g fat (of which approximately 5.5 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 5.2 g carbohydrate. The estimated glycemic index is low (~45), making it suitable for blood sugar management when portion-controlled. Sodium content is moderate at 280 mg per 100 g (~700 mg per serving), warranting attention for hypertension management. The dish is a staple at Malay weddings (kenduri) and festive occasions and is typically served with Nasi Minyak (ghee rice) or tomato rice. Its mild flavour makes it popular with children and those who prefer non-spicy cuisine. Clinically, the high protein and fat content attenuate postprandial glucose response, and the kurma spice blend contributes anti-inflammatory phytochemicals.

confidence_reason: Ayam Masak Kurma is not listed as a discrete entry in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved-source analogues (HPB Malay-style chicken curry with coconut milk; IMR Malaysia MyFCD similar coconut-milk chicken stews). Core macros are consistent with the input record and the adapted source range. Saturated fat is estimated. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Ayam Masak Kurma are not available as discrete entries in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2024. 2. saturated_fat_g (5.5 g per 100 g) is estimated based on the typical coconut milk content of this dish class, consistent with HPB and MyFCD reference ranges for similar Malay coconut-milk chicken stews. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using mild coconut-milk chicken curry / chicken korma as the reference food; no direct GI measurement for Ayam Masak Kurma exists in any approved GI source. 4. Glycemic load per serving = (45 × 13.0 g carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 5.9. 5. All core macros (protein_g 9.8, fat_g 10.5, carbohydrate_g 5.2) are non-zero and sourced from HPB-adapted values; no zero-macro justification is required. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for Ayam Masak Kurma in FoodOn, FoodEx2, or SNOMED CT as of 2024. 7. The dish is generally considered halal as prepared with chicken and plant-based spices; yogurt and cashew variants should be verified for halal certification.
